items.name: Taxi Driver
items.reason: Scorsese's fever dream captures the psychic rot beneath the city's neon decay, where alienation stops being mood and becomes the weather itself.

items.name: The Friends of Eddie Coyle
items.reason: Peter Yates's crime film shares the same exhausted masculinity and urban corrosion, showing a city that does not contain violence so much as manufacture it.

items.name: Suicide
items.reason: Alan Vega and Martin Rev turned cheap electronics into downtown menace, making music that sounded less performed than exhaled by the city itself.

items.name: Frankie Teardrop
items.reason: Ten minutes of factory-floor panic and domestic collapse, like the sound of the American Dream cracking open in a Lower East Side hallway.

items.name: The Power Broker
items.reason: Caro shows how Robert Moses built the physical systems that later decayed into 70s New York, turning policy into a hidden prehistory of urban ruin.

items.name: Co-op City
items.reason: This Bronx megaproject embodies the era's busted utopianism, a vertical suburb where planning ambition hardened into concrete estrangement.

items.name: New York Dolls at the Mercer Arts Center Collapse
items.reason: A glam-rock venue literally caving in feels like the perfect emblem of the period, when every cultural breakthrough seemed balanced on rotten infrastructure.

items.name: Oscar Wilde Room
items.reason: This tiny Mercer basement was the larval stage of punk, a room where glam collapse and downtown reinvention still looked like the same scene.

searchHint.title: Oscar Wilde Room Mercer Arts Center

items.name: Alphabet City
items.reason: The neighborhood east of Avenue A became the city's sacrifice zone, where artists, junkies, and families all learned to live inside abandonment.

items.name: Loisaida
items.reason: The Puerto Rican name for the Lower East Side preserves the community imagination that survived even as the neighborhood around it was left to crumble.

items.name: Colson Whitehead
items.reason: Whitehead grew up inside the afterimage of this city and later wrote its decay as a mental map, not just a historical condition.

items.name: The Colossus of New York
items.reason: Whitehead's essay-book turns sidewalks, subways, and towers into memory devices, proving the ruined city never stopped shaping the people who survived it.
